questions.question: Why did Akbar adopt a policy of matrimonial alliances with the Rajputs?
questions.answer: Akbar adopted a policy of matrimonial alliances with the Rajputs because he realised that their support was essential for building a large and stable empire. The Rajputs were powerful warriors, and by forming alliances rather than fighting them, Akbar could win their loyalty and service. This policy helped him gain brave soldiers and administrators like Raja Man Singh and Raja Birbal, while also ensuring peace across Rajasthan.

questions.question: Why did Akbar remove Bairam Khan from the position of regent?
questions.answer: Akbar removed Bairam Khan because over the four years of his regency (1556-1560), Bairam Khan had become arrogant and had started appointing his favourite nobles to important positions. A group of nobles at court opposed him. When Akbar turned 18, he decided to take charge of the administration himself and asked Bairam Khan to go on a pilgrimage to Mecca.

questions.question: Why did Akbar abolish the jizya and pilgrim tax on Hindus?
questions.answer: Akbar abolished the jizya and pilgrim tax on Hindus in 1562 because he held liberal religious views and believed in the basic unity of all religions. These taxes were unfair and imposed only on Hindus, causing resentment. By abolishing them, Akbar promoted religious harmony and won the goodwill and loyalty of his Hindu subjects, which helped strengthen his empire.

questions.question: Why was the conquest of Gujarat important for Akbar?
questions.answer: The conquest of Gujarat in 1572 was important for Akbar because Gujarat had a rich cotton industry, important seaports, and thriving trade. Controlling Gujarat gave the Mughal empire access to lucrative foreign trade routes and the wealth generated by its textile industry and commerce, significantly strengthening the empire's economic power.

questions.question: Why did Raja Todar Mal replace ropes with bamboo sticks (jarib) for land measurement?
questions.answer: Raja Todar Mal replaced ropes with bamboo sticks called jarib (fitted with iron rings) because ropes were unreliable for land measurement. Ropes would expand when wet and contract when dry, leading to inaccurate measurements. Bamboo sticks with iron rings maintained a consistent length regardless of weather conditions, ensuring fair and accurate assessment of land for revenue purposes.

questions.question: Why did Akbar build Fatehpur Sikri as his new capital?
questions.answer: Akbar built Fatehpur Sikri as his new capital in 1575 because the Sufi saint Salim Chisti lived at that site near Agra. Akbar had great reverence for the saint. He used the new capital as a place to invite religious scholars from different faiths - Hindus, Jains, Parsis, and Christians - for discussions, which eventually led to his suggestion of Din-i-Ilahi.

questions.question: Why was the mansab rank not made hereditary by Akbar?
questions.answer: Akbar did not make the mansab rank hereditary because he wanted to prevent the growth of a feudal class that could challenge imperial authority. By ensuring that after a mansabdar's death, his jagir and rank reverted to the emperor, Akbar maintained direct control over all appointments. This system kept the emperor supreme and prevented any noble family from accumulating too much power over generations.

questions.question: Why did Din-i-Ilahi fade out after Akbar's death?
questions.answer: Din-i-Ilahi faded out after Akbar's death in 1605 because it was not a formal religion but rather a Sufi-like order that depended heavily on Akbar's personal authority as the spiritual guide. Akbar had not forced anyone to follow it, so it had very few followers. Even trusted officials like Raja Man Singh had refused it. Without Akbar's personal presence and influence, there was no one to sustain the movement, and it gradually disappeared.
